checks.a_analysis: The conversion faithfully represents the text found in the source document. All headings, tables, and list items in the output have a clear basis in the provided source images and OCR text. No fabricated content was discovered.
No contact information such as phone numbers, email addresses, or physical addresses is present in either the source document or the generated output. Consequently, no fabrication has occurred.

All three pages of the source PDF are accounted for in the JSON output. Page 1 contains the 2024 collection data and map; Page 2 contains the 2025 collection area map and legend; Page 3 contains the 2025 collection area map and the list of project details. Each of these is present in the output.

The output contains tables for the map legends. The 'Phase & Quality' table on Page 1 correctly includes all 5 data rows. The legend table for the 2025 map on Page 2 correctly includes all 4 categories listed in the source image. No rows are missing.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: While there is a minor omission of map labels on the first page, the vast majority of the textual content (headings, full paragraphs, and list items) has been captured perfectly. The omissions do not reach the threshold of 25% of any section's text content.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: There is a minor omission of specific data from Page 1. The map callout labels 'QL 1 data collection completed' and 'Data available February 2025' are present in the source and the provided OCR text but are completely absent from the structured text elements in the JSON output. Furthermore, this specific date ('February 2025') is not captured in the image alt text, resulting in an information loss for users relying on the text stream.
checks.b_evidence_source: QL 1 data collection completed / Data available February 2025
checks.b_evidence_output: Not present in text elements (paragraphs or tables) or image alt text.

checks.a_analysis: I examined the output for any instances where text blocks were repeated more often than in the source. The title '2025 Data Collection Area' appears on both pages 2 and 3 of the source and is correctly reflected twice in the output. No extraneous or erroneous duplication of paragraphs, lists, or tables was found.

The heading hierarchy is consistent. Each section starts with a Heading 1. Section 3 uses two Heading 1 tags, where 'North Carolina Collaboratory' is a subtitle in the source. This adjustment from a subtitle to a heading level is considered a normalized heading level for accessibility and is acceptable. There are no skipped levels (e.g., H1 directly to H3).

The document contains graphic legends that have been converted into tables. I reviewed the table structures at reading orders 3 and 14. They accurately represent the content and organization of the legends found on pages 1 and 2 of the source PDF. Column and row organization is preserved.
The reading order follows the logical flow of the source document. Text elements, tables (legends), and images are sequenced in a manner that reflects the page-by-page layout of the PDF.

The bulleted list on page 3 of the source document is correctly identified and converted as a list structure in the JSON output (reading order 22). Each bullet point is preserved as a distinct list item.
There are no hyperlinks present in the source document, and none have been erroneously added to the JSON output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization of the source PDF. Content from each of the three pages is grouped into logical sections. Legends from the maps have been correctly converted into accessible tables while the map images themselves remain. The hierarchical structure of headings and subheadings reflects the source's intent. Although the bulleted list on Page 3 is moved from a side-by-side layout with the map to a vertical layout, this is a standard and acceptable change for responsive HTML design.

The legends from the maps have been converted into tables. These tables (e.g., the 'Phase & Quality' table for the first map and the legend table for the Western NC map) are rendered with clear rows and legible text. They successfully convey the information contained in the original graphical legends.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes all three map variations (the statewide phase map and the two Western NC topographic maps), the LiDAR plane diagram, and all agency logos (DPS, Emergency Management, EMAP). All images are rendered correctly without broken links.

There is no unintended duplication of body content. While the Western NC map appears twice and agency logos appear in each section, this is a faithful representation of the source document where these elements are repeated across different pages. The conversion correctly maps each page's unique content to its corresponding section in the HTML.
